primary_diagnosis.name: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis with acute bulbar decompensation — high aspiration risk, impending neuromuscular respiratory compromise and falls risk (acute-on-chronic)

pathophysiology_summary: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis is a progressive degeneration of both upper and lower motor neurons, producing the mixed UMN (hyperreflexia, extensor plantar) and LMN (atrophy, fasciculation, flaccid weakness) picture, plus bulbar failure (dysarthria, dysphagia, poor secretion clearance). The acute problem here is decompensation of that trajectory: bulbar weakness has failed to protect the airway, causing a choking episode on saliva and creating a high risk of aspiration and aspiration pneumonia; progressive diaphragmatic/respiratory-muscle weakness (shallow excursion, resting tachypnoea, borderline hypoxia) threatens neuromuscular ventilatory failure; and worsening limb weakness threatens injurious falls. The underlying disease is known and confirmed — the clinical task is to recognise the NEW acute complications superimposed on it, not to re-diagnose the ALS. Secretion-drying agents (hyoscine, atropine, glycopyrrolate) and swallowing/positioning measures (thickened fluids, chin-tuck) reduce aspiration risk symptomatically but do not reverse the neurodegeneration; respiratory support (NIV) and advance care planning are the trajectory-level responses.

differential.diagnosis: Acute bulbar/aspiration decompensation of known motor neurone disease
differential.evidence_basis: Known ALS with a new choking episode on saliva, wet voice and secretion pooling — bulbar failure decompensating.
Choking on secretions with resting tachypnoea and borderline hypoxia — aspiration pneumonitis/pneumonia must be actively excluded.
Shallow breathing, RR 22, SpO2 94%, anxiety about breathing muscles in a disease that kills through respiratory failure.
New weakness and dysarthria can suggest stroke, but must be weighed against a 3-week progressive course and widespread fasciculations.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI Doctor should treat the acute complication (aspiration/respiratory risk), not re-litigate the underlying diagnosis, which the patient already carries.

differential.diagnosis: Acute bulbar/aspiration decompensation of known ALS
differential.evidence_basis: Progressive over weeks with a sharp step-down today; bulbar failure and secretion pooling on a confirmed ALS background.
No fever or purulent sputum YET, but the aspiration event plus tachypnoea/hypoxia mandate active screening and a low threshold for chest imaging.
Chronic progressive asymmetric weakness with widespread fasciculations and no sudden dense hemiparesis or field deficit argues strongly against acute stroke.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Stroke can be reasonably de-prioritised on the temporal course; the aspiration and respiratory complications cannot be de-prioritised.

differential.diagnosis: ALS with acute bulbar decompensation, high aspiration and respiratory risk
differential.evidence_basis: Bulbar red flags (choking on saliva, wet voice), respiratory red flags (shallow breathing, tachypnoea, hypoxia) and falls red flags all present.
UMN signs (hyperreflexia, extensor plantar) and absence of fluctuating ocular/fatigable weakness argue against myasthenia.
Prominent bulbar involvement (dysarthria, tongue fasciculation) localises pathology above the cervical cord.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The named mimics from the source (stroke, MG, cervical myelopathy) are all sealed answer-key reasoning and must not surface in the presentation layer.

differential.diagnosis: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis with acute bulbar decompensation — aspiration risk, impending neuromuscular respiratory compromise, falls risk
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed ALS decompensating on multiple fronts; requires urgent in-person assessment for aspiration/respiratory status plus symptom optimisation and advance care planning.

red_flags.description: Unprovoked choking episode on saliva with prolonged coughing — bulbar airway-protection failure
red_flags.clinical_significance: Loss of airway protection risks aspiration and aspiration pneumonia; a further episode that does not clear can be immediately life-threatening.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Aspiration pneumonia or fatal airway obstruction on a subsequent choking episode.

red_flags.description: Resting tachypnoea (RR ~22), borderline hypoxia (SpO2 94%) and shallow breathing — impending neuromuscular respiratory failure
red_flags.clinical_significance: ALS ultimately causes death through respiratory-muscle failure; these are early warning signs that ventilatory reserve is falling and NIV/respiratory review may be needed.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised hypoventilation, CO2 retention and respiratory arrest.

red_flags.description: Rapidly worsening limb weakness with near-falls today
red_flags.clinical_significance: High risk of injurious falls (fracture, head injury) in a home not adapted for his mobility aid.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Major traumatic injury from an unmitigated fall.

red_flags.description: Established or evolving aspiration pneumonia (fever, productive cough, rising work of breathing)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Currently no fever or purulent sputum, but the aspiration event places him at high risk over the next 24-48 hours; this is the diagnostic-overshadowing trap.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to sepsis or respiratory failure if a developing pneumonia is dismissed as 'just his ALS'.

red_flags.description: High carer strain and unsafe home environment
red_flags.clinical_significance: Carer burnout and environmental hazards compound falls and aspiration risk and threaten the sustainability of home care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Care breakdown, further falls, delayed help-seeking.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the new breathlessness, tachypnoea and choking to 'just the ALS' and failing to screen for a treatable superimposed aspiration pneumonia or acute respiratory decompensation. This is the primary error of omission the case probes.
Spending the encounter re-working the underlying diagnosis (which is already confirmed) instead of recognising and triaging the acute complication.

Trying to manage acute bulbar/respiratory decompensation entirely at home over chat, when the safe action is urgent in-person assessment because airway, respiratory and aspiration status cannot be examined remotely.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: False reassurance from a settled episode
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being reassured that the choking has stopped and the patient is 'stable', and under-triaging to a routine review — the risk of a further episode and of evolving pneumonia remains high.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Auscultation of the chest / true air entry and any crackles of aspiration
Objective vital signs without a device (measured RR, HR 102, BP 110/70, temperature)
Neurological examination (hyperreflexia, right extensor plantar/Babinski, upper-limb hyporeflexia and flaccid weakness, tongue fasciculation, degree of muscle atrophy)
Diaphragmatic excursion and true respiratory-muscle reserve (FVC / sniff nasal inspiratory pressure)
Objective swallow assessment and chest imaging for aspiration
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise acute bulbar/respiratory decompensation of known ALS and arrange URGENT same-day in-person clinical assessment (GP home visit / community rapid-response / ED if respiratory signs worsen) to evaluate aspiration and respiratory status; give safe swallowing and positioning advice; escalate to 000 for any choking not clearing within 1 minute, cyanosis, or severe breathlessness at rest.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The underlying diagnosis is not the risk (it is known). The risk is UNDER-action: dismissing the acute complications as chronic disease and deferring to a routine next-week review, or attempting full home management of an airway/respiratory problem that cannot be examined remotely.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NICE NG42 — Motor neurone disease: assessment and management
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Proactive respiratory monitoring, secretion management, and urgent review of new respiratory or swallowing decline; NIV consideration for respiratory-muscle weakness.
Multidisciplinary management of dysphagia and sialorrhoea; aspiration-prevention measures and advance care planning.
